julia> struct D
       dict::Dict{Symbol,Any}
       end
       Base.getproperty(A::AbstractArray{<:D}, f::Symbol) = getindex.(getfield.(A, :dict), f)

julia> D() = D(Dict())
D

julia> [D()]

@nsajko I accept that this is type-piracy, but I take it then that this is a kind of 'undefined behaviour' and we cannot expect it to work?

> The specific reason why your usage fails is that you hijack Array's getproperty, making its (private) fields inaccessible. Perhaps it should be documented that adding a getproperty method is only allowed when one controls the fields.

Yes, I realise that and in the past, because `Array` was implemented in C, there were no `getproperty(::Array, ::Symbol)` calls in Base anywhere.  Perhaps `Base` can use `getfield(::Array, ::Symbol)` for field access instead?  That would seem to make it less susceptible to user error.  I count roughly 80 uses of `array.ref` in the Julia source tree, and I'd be happy to submit a PR to replace those with `getfield(a, :ref)` (or a function which does that), and similarly for `array.size`.

> That particular issue is easily fixable, like this (just remove the <:):
>
> ```Base.getproperty(A::AbstractArray{D}, f::Symbol) = getindex.(getfield.(A, :dict), f)```

Thank you for the suggestion, though it I guess for the `UnionAll` reason it doesn't work for paramerised types:

```
julia> struct A{V}
           a::V
       end
       Base.getproperty(a::AbstractArray{A{V}}, f::Symbol) where V = getfield.(a, f)
       [A(1), A(1.0)].a

I had certainly expected that `typemin(DateTime)` represented the earliest possible DateTime that you can make—but it appears that is not the case at all.  In fact you can make a `DateTime` in a straightforward way that is less than `typemin(DateTime)` without resorting to any trickery:

```julia
julia> DateTime(-146138511 - 1) < typemin(DateTime)
true
```

I didn't realise that the _true_ minimum date was so much earlier!

```julia
julia> DateTime(Dates.UTInstant(Millisecond(typemin(Int64))))
-292277024-05-15T16:47:04.192
```

It must be said that I have never had a problem because most of my data were recorded from the 20th century at the latest, but you never know for someone else's workflow.  However I would view any change here as a bugfix, since the documented API as I read it of `typemin` and `typemax` suggest something different to what happens.

By the way, for `Date` the situation appears to be similar:

```julia
julia> typemin(Date) - Day(1)
-252522163911151-12-31

julia> typemin(Date)
-252522163911150-01-01
```

However the behaviour is a bit funky when you pass `typemin(Date)` far enough!  (Note that in the second line the wrapped `Dates.UTInstant{Day}` hasn't itself wrapped around yet—_i.e._, is greater than `typemin(Day)`.)

```julia
julia> typemin(Date) - Day(1000)
252522163911148--6028347736506382--23

julia> (typemin(Date) - Day(1000)).instant
Dates.UTInstant{Day}(Day(-92231826452319568))
```

This makes me think that it should be impossible to create `Date`s or `DateTime`s smaller or larger than that which `typemin`/`typemax` returns.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1681245233/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This is a proposal for fixing the linked issue. The PR consists of two commits. 

The first commit just adds a few tests and one sentence to the docs of `DateTime`. I think this could be merged.

The second commit changes addition/subtraction of a `DateTime` and `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond`. 
Before:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
After:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 microsecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 nanosecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
The second commit is breaking so I am not sure if it can be merged.

No-one as far as we know relies on being able to make `Date`s or `DateTime`s which lie outside the range given by `typemin`/`typemax`, and those values were chosen to potentially avoid other problems.  Can we then just enforce `Date`s and `DateTime`s to be in that range in the inner constructor?

In `types.jl`:
```
# Before the Date and DateTime definitions
const TYPEMIN_DATETIME = UTInstant{Millisecond}(Millisecond(-4611686008435200000))
const TYPEMAX_DATETIME = UTInstant{Millisecond}(Millisecond(4611686008607999000))
const TYPEMIN_DATE = UTInstant{Day}(Day(-92231826452318568))
const TYPEMAX_DATE = Dates.UTInstant{Day}(Day(92231826452317838))

# ...

struct Date <: TimeType
    instant::UTInstant{Day}
    function Date(instant::UTInstant{Day})
        TYPEMIN_DATE <= instant <= TYPEMAX_DATE ||
            throw(ArgumentError("instant lies outside the range representable by a Date")
        new(instant)
    end
end

# And likewise for DateTime
```

I don't have a feel for how much slower this might be (maybe not very?), and it doesn't properly handle true under/overflow of the `UTInstant`, but it does at least mean that what `typemin` and `typemax` mean is consistent with the values that any function taking `Date`s or `DateTime`s expect.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1683631232/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> Nice example! There is no reason we couldn't define this to be +(::Date, ::Hour) -> DateTime

@timholy From my point of view, the reason not to do this is that a my understanding of a `Date` is that it is a specific day in history, not any particular time on that day (i.e., not 00:00). It is
meant to be used to help in making the decision whether the change
can be made in a minor Julia release, or really does need to
wait for a major version change.  The commit message follows:

---

When attempting to construct a `DateTime`, `Date` or `Time` from an
`AbstractString`, throw an `ArgumentError` if the string is empty.
Likewise, error when `parse`ing an empty string as one of these types,
and return `nothing` from `tryparse`.

This behavior differs from previously.  Before, `Date` and `Time` would
return default values of `Date(1)` and `Time(0)`, respectively, while
`DateTime` would error without a `format` argument.  With a `format`
argument, it would return `DateTime(1)`.  However, this appears not to
have been explicitly intended, but rather a consequence of the way
parsing was implemented; no tests for empty string parsing existed.

This addresses #28090 and #43883; see discussion therein.

Summary of changes:
- Check for empty string in `Base.parse(::DateTime)` and throw if so.
- Change documentation to mention this.
- Add a compat notice to the docs contrasting the old behavior.

PR is an RFC relating to the discussion in #43883.  It is
meant to be used to help in making the decision whether the change
can be made in a minor Julia release, or really does need to
wait for a major version change.  The commit message follows:

---

When attempting to construct a `DateTime`, `Date` or `Time` from an
`AbstractString`, throw an `ArgumentError` if the string is empty.
Likewise, error when `parse`ing an empty string as one of these types,
and return `nothing` from `tryparse`.

This behavior differs from previously.  Before, `Date` and `Time` would
return default values of `Date(1)` and `Time(0)`, respectively, while
`DateTime` would error without a `format` argument.  With a `format`
argument, it would return `DateTime(1)`.  However, this appears not to
have been explicitly intended, but rather a consequence of the way
parsing was implemented; no tests for empty string parsing existed.

This addresses #28090 and #43883; see discussion therein.

Summary of changes:
- Check for empty string in `Base.parse(::DateTime)` and throw if so.
- Change documentation to mention this.
- Add a compat notice to the docs contrasting the old behavior.
